metadata.raw_response_text:
# Give the Bus Its Own Lane

Every morning, thousands of commuters sit trapped in the same ritual humiliation: a forty-minute crawl down a corridor that should take ten. Their cars inch forward. Their blood pressure climbs. And somewhere in the middle of that gridlock, a city bus carrying sixty people sits stuck in exactly the same jam, punished for doing the mathematically correct thing.

It is time to stop punishing the bus. It is time for dedicated bus lanes.

The argument against them is always the same, and it is always stated with great conviction by someone who has never actually done the math. "You're removing a lane of traffic," goes the objection. "You'll make congestion worse." This sounds reasonable until you consider what a lane of traffic actually carries. A single lane of mixed urban traffic moves somewhere between 600 and 1,600 vehicles per hour. A dedicated bus lane, running frequent service, can move upward of 8,000 people in that same space in the same time. The lane doesn't carry fewer people when you paint it red. It carries dramatically more.

Congestion is not caused by too little road space. It is caused by too many single-occupant vehicles competing for the space that exists. Adding capacity for cars — the approach we have tried for seventy years — reliably fills with new car traffic within a few years, a phenomenon so well-documented that transportation planners have a name for it: induced demand. You cannot build your way out of gridlock. But you can offer people a fast, reliable alternative that makes driving the less attractive option. That is precisely what a working bus rapid transit corridor does.

Consider what "fast and reliable" actually requires. Right now, the bus on this corridor stops at every red light, merges through the same bottlenecks, and gets stuck behind left-turning SUVs. It is slow not because buses are inherently slow, but because we have engineered slowness into the system by forcing buses to compete on equal terms with a mode of transport they were never designed to compete with. A dedicated lane, combined with transit signal priority — technology that holds a green light slightly longer as a bus approaches — transforms journey times. Cities that have implemented bus rapid transit corridors consistently report travel time reductions of 20 to 40 percent. Ridership rises. Operating costs per passenger fall. The system becomes self-reinforcing.

The equity argument is equally compelling, and it rarely receives the attention it deserves. The people most dependent on public transit are typically those with the least access to cars: lower-income workers, the elderly, people with disabilities, recent immigrants, students. When we design our roads exclusively around the automobile, we are designing them around a tool that a significant portion of the city's population cannot use or cannot afford. Dedicating a lane to the bus is not a gift to transit riders at the expense of drivers. It is an acknowledgment that the people in that bus have exactly as much right to move efficiently through the city as anyone else — more right, arguably, given how little road space they collectively consume.

Opponents will raise the issue of business access, and that concern deserves a serious answer. Evidence from cities that have implemented bus lanes — London, Bogotá, Brisbane, Los Angeles — consistently shows that retail corridors with high-quality transit access perform as well or better than comparable car-oriented corridors. Customers who arrive by bus spend more time and, over the course of a week, often as much money as those who arrive by car. Removing the friction of transit access tends to increase foot traffic, not diminish it.

None of this requires pretending the transition is painless. Some drivers will have a worse commute in the short term. Some loading and parking arrangements will need to change. Political will is required, and political will is exactly what these proposals tend to encounter resistance to expending. It is much easier to do nothing, to let the gridlock calcify, to keep promising studies and consultations and pilot programs that quietly expire.

But doing nothing has a cost too. It is just a cost distributed invisibly — in lost hours, in diesel emissions, in the slow economic suffocation of a city that cannot move its people efficiently, in the daily indignity handed to the sixty people on that bus while the corridor chokes around them.

Give the bus its own lane. Count the people, not the vehicles. Build the city around movement, not around cars.

The math has been done. The only question is whether we have the nerve to act on it.
